title: A New Machine That Can Study Billions of Molecules at Once
summary: Scientists use a tool called mass spectrometry to find out what molecules are in a sample, like in cells or chemicals. Most machines today can only look at one or a few molecules at a time, which is slow and might miss rare ones. Researchers have made a new prototype called MultiQ-IT that can handle billions of molecules together, inspired by how cells move things through many small openings. This could help in studying single cells better, watching many reactions at once, and making drug discovery faster. The idea came from improving technology, similar to how computers and DNA sequencing got better by doing many things in parallel. This new device uses a cube with hundreds of openings to sort and analyze ions, which are charged molecules, all at the same time, making the process much more efficient.

molecules that have an electric charge, used in mass spectrometry

background_read: Mass spectrometry is a scientific tool used for many years to study molecules by giving them a charge and measuring them. It helps in biology and chemistry, but old machines are slow because they check molecules one by one. This new prototype, MultiQ-IT, aims to make it faster by handling billions together, similar to how computers and DNA sequencing improved. Understanding this can help learn about cells and develop new medicines more quickly.
Article_Structure: WHO: Scientists and researchers, including Brian T. Chait and Andrew Krutchinsky
WHAT: Developed a new prototype called MultiQ-IT that can process billions of molecules at once
WHERE: In laboratories, such as at Rockefeller University
WHY: To make mass spectrometry faster, more sensitive, and better at detecting rare molecules for applications like single-cell studies and drug discovery

perspectives.description: The researchers see this as a way to improve technology by learning from other fields like computing and biology.
